revision.text:
{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|qq=}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|qq=}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural landmarks in Japan]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[en:Narabigaoka]]

Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural landmarks in Japan]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[en:Narabigaoka]] [[Category:京都府にある国指定の名勝]] [[Category:京都府の自然景勝地]] [[Category:卜部兼好]] [[Category:右京区の地理]]

{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|qq=}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|qq=}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural landmarks in Japan]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[en:Narabigaoka]] [[Category:京都府にある国指定の名勝]] [[Category:京都府の自然景勝地]] [[Category:卜部兼好]] [[Category:右京区の地理]] [[Category:右京区の歴史]]

The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural landmarks in Japan]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[en:Narabigaoka]] [[Category:京都府にある国指定の名勝]] [[Category:京都府の自然景勝地]] [[Category:卜部兼好]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:山岳名目録]]

{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|qq=}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[Category:京都府にある国指定の名勝]] [[Category:京都府の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}}

After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[Category:京都府にある国指定の名勝]] [[Category:京都府の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}}

After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q18337635]] Wikidata name: Narabigaoka [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|qq=}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Narabigaoka]] [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:arabigaoka]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}}

It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|qq=|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}}

Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18337635]] Wikidata name: Narabigaoka [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18337635]] Wikidata name: Narabigaoka [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18337635]] Wikidata name: Narabigaoka [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]]

(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto]], Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, [[:en:Yoshida Kenkō|Urabe no Kanekuni]], spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myoshinji Temple]] to the east and [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninnaji Temple]] to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to [[:en:Hōkongō-in|Hokongoin Temple]]. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18337635]] Wikidata name: Narabigaoka [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, {{ill|Yoshida Kenkō|en|Yoshida Kenkō|WD=Q372772|lt=Urabe no Kanekuni}}, spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myoshinji Temple}} to the east and {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|WD=Q1202871|lt=Ninnaji Temple}} to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to {{ill|Hōkongō-in|en|Hōkongō-in|WD=Q11143738|lt=Hokongoin Temple}}. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[sn:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, {{ill|Yoshida Kenkō|en|Yoshida Kenkō|WD=Q372772|lt=Urabe no Kanekuni}}, spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myoshinji Temple}} to the east and {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|WD=Q1202871|lt=Ninnaji Temple}} to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to {{ill|Hōkongō-in|en|Hōkongō-in|WD=Q11143738|lt=Hokongoin Temple}}. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:qq]]

[[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[co:commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] [[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Narabigaoka]][[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki region|Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto City|Ukyo Ward|frame=1}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Narabigaoka | image = [[File:Narabigaoka140721NI2.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = Narabigaoka seen from the foothills of {{ill|Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|ja|衣笠山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugasa Mountain (Kyoto)|1=kinugasayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9034746}}
(From right: First Hill, Second Hill, Third Hill) | elevation = 116.2 m{{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058-1059 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|1|22|N|135|42|48|E|region:JP-26}} | location = {{Flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho | map = {{location map |Japan Kyoto city#Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan |relief = 1 |float = center |lat_deg = 35 |lat_min = 1 |lat_sec = 22 |lat_dir = N |lon_deg = 135 |lon_min = 42 |lon_sec = 48 |lon_dir = E |width = 250 |display = inline,title |label = Narabigaoka }} | OSM_zoom = 12 }} '''Narabigaoka''' (双ヶ丘) is an isolated hill made of Paleozoic strata {{efn2|Geological strata formed during the Paleozoic Era}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Omuro Narabioka-cho. Designated a national {{ill|Scenic spot|ja|名勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic spot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Place of Scenic Beauty|WD=Q11414752}}, it rises to 116 meters in the northwest part of the Kyoto Basin. It is known as the place where the author of *Tsurezuregusa*, {{ill|Yoshida Kenkō|en|Yoshida Kenkō|WD=Q372772|lt=Urabe no Kanekuni|1=Yoshida Kenkō}}, spent his later years. == Name Variations == Narabigaoka is referred to by various names, including 双ヶ丘, 雙ヶ岡, 双ヶ岡, 双岡, 並岡, 雙丘, and 双岳. * '''双ヶ丘''': Used by institutions like Kyoto Municipal Narabigaoka Junior High School and Kyoto City Bus stops such as Narabigaoka. * '''雙ヶ岡''': Official designation as a national scenic spot; also used by Kyoto City and local tourist resources. * '''双ヶ岡''': Used for the Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster; appears in the *Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary*. * '''双岡''': Used by Kyoto Narabioka Hospital and for addresses in Ukyo Ward. == Geography == [[File:Ukyo-ku & Mount Hiei (3811327584).jpg|thumb|Narabigaoka viewed from the southwest, with Mount Hiei in the background]] [[File:Narabigaoka aerial photo 20200819.jpg|thumb|Aerial view (2020)]] Narabigaoka consists of three hills aligned north to south: the First Hill (116 m), Second Hill (102 m), and Third Hill (78 m). The area’s average elevation is about 40–70 meters at nearby landmarks such as JR Hanazono Station and Keifuku Omuro-Ninnaji Station. The total area of the hills is approximately 18.9 hectares. Roads such as Marutamachi-dori run along the southern foothills, and National Route 162 borders the western side. Adjacent landmarks include {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myoshinji Temple|1=Myōshin-ji}} to the east and {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|WD=Q1202871|lt=Ninnaji Temple|1=Ninna-ji}} to the north. Between the Third Hill and the nearby Goinzan Hill lies the historical Narabi Pond, now drained and urbanized. == Historical Importance == === Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster === [[File:Narabigaoka 1go-fun, zenkei.JPG|thumb|{{center|Narabigaoka No. 1 Kofun}}]] Narabigaoka is home to 24 Kofuns constructed between the late 6th and early 7th centuries, collectively known as the {{ill|Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja|双ヶ岡古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narabigaoka Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 双ヶ岡1号墳|1=Narabigaoka 1 Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123120845}}. The First Hill hosts the largest tumulus, No. 1 Kofun, a 44-meter diameter round Kofun with a 15.8-meter-long horizontal stone chamber. The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to {{ill|Hōkongō-in|en|Hōkongō-in|WD=Q11143738|lt=Hokongoin Temple|1=Hōkongō-in}}. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:双ヶ丘]] {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635}} [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]] [[Category:pages]]

The cluster is linked to the Hata clan, who are believed to have used the site for burial purposes. === Residence of Nobility === During the Heian period, Narabigaoka was used as a hunting ground for emperors and housed aristocratic villas. Notably, {{ill|Kiyohara no Natsuno|ja|清原夏野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Natsuno|1=Kiyohara no Natsuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11560500}} built a villa there, which was visited by Emperor Junna. After Natsuno’s death, the villa became the precursor to {{ill|Hōkongō-in|en|Hōkongō-in|WD=Q11143738|lt=Hokongoin Temple|1=Hōkongō-in}}. == References == * {{Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Editorial Board 1982}}, pp.1058–1059 * {{KyotoCity}} Kyoto Tourism Office, 2011 == External Links == * [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005651.html Narabigaoka Information (Kyoto City)] {{translated page|ja|双ヶ丘|version=101145930|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Commons category|Narabigaoka}} {{wikidata link|Q18337635|co|commons:Category:Narabigaoka|ja|双ヶ丘}} [[Category:Geography of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Ukyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Mountains of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:National places of scenic beauty in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Natural scenic spots of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Yoshida Kenko]]
